name: Nazi Bell Die Glocke
description: Die Glocke (The Bell) is a purported top-secret Nazi scientific technological device, object, or experiment that was said to have been conducted by Nazi Germany near the end of World War II. Described by Polish journalist and author Igor Witkowski in 2000, it has since been popularized in English-language conspiracy theories and alternative history publications. According to these accounts, Die Glocke was a device made of a hard, metallic substance, approximately 9 feet wide and 12 feet high, with a shape similar to a large bell. Proponents claim the device contained two counter-rotating cylinders filled with a substance code-named 'Xerum 525', and that experiments with it resulted in effects such as animal death, plant decomposition, and the disintegration of crystalline structures. Some theories suggest Die Glocke was intended as a time machine, teleportation device, or anti-gravity weapon. Mainstream historians and scientists dismiss Die Glocke as a hoax or a misinterpretation of real, but mundane, wartime research. No physical evidence of Die Glocke has ever been found, and most accounts rely on anecdotal evidence and interpretations of declassified documents. The lack of credible evidence, coupled with the fantastical nature of the claims, leads the scientific community to view the Die Glocke narratives as pseudo-science and speculation.
subtitle: Alleged Nazi Wonder Weapon Conspiracy

claims.claim: Die Glocke was a highly advanced Nazi device.
claims.evidence: Igor Witkowski's book and witness testimonies (often anonymous).
claims.criticism: Lack of physical evidence, reliance on hearsay, and the absence of corroborating documentation.

claims.claim: The device possessed anti-gravity or time-altering capabilities.
claims.evidence: Alleged effects observed during experiments, such as distorted time perception and gravitational anomalies.
claims.criticism: These effects are not scientifically plausible based on the purported design and materials, and lack empirical verification.

claims.claim: Die Glocke project was moved to South America after the war.
claims.evidence: Speculation based on alleged sightings and unexplained phenomena in South America.
claims.criticism: No reliable evidence supports this claim, and it is based on conjecture and unsubstantiated rumors.

keyFigures.name: Igor Witkowski
keyFigures.role: Author who popularized the Die Glocke myth.

keyFigures.name: Nick Cook
keyFigures.role: Aviation editor who investigated the claims but remained skeptical.

keyFigures.name: Joseph P. Farrell
keyFigures.role: Author who further developed the Die Glocke conspiracy theory.

timeline.event: Alleged development and testing of Die Glocke.
Publication of Igor Witkowski's book popularizing the legend.
Proliferation of Die Glocke theories in alternative history and conspiracy communities.

sources.author: Brian Dunning
sources.work: Skeptoid Podcast #36: Die Glocke: The Nazi Bell
sources.title: Brian Dunning
